essays.body_html:
What this essay does

This essay traces the constitutional-doctrinal arc of the Israeli Supreme Court from a foundational 1995 ruling to a peak moment in January 2024 — a thirty-year period during which the Court substantially expanded its own authority over Israel’s elected institutions. The empirical record is drawn from this project’s documentary core (data/rulings/) and from sources cited throughout.

The interpretive frame is Prof. Moshe Cohen-Eliya’s juristocracy thesis as articulated in Israel’s Juristocracy (Law & Liberty, March 2024) and in the broader work of the constitutional-conservatism scholars he is most closely associated with (Iddo Porat, Moshe Koppel, Yaakov Ben Shemesh). The opposing view, anchored in Ran Hirschl’s Towards Juristocracy (Harvard, 2004) and in defenders of the constitutional-revolution architecture, is engaged in adversarial fairness.

This is a documentary essay. It traces what happened, with citations. It does not attempt to settle the question of whether the developments documented were normatively good or bad. That question is properly decided in democratic deliberation; what this essay provides is the structured record of what is to be deliberated.

The starting point: a constitution that was never written

Israel was founded in 1948 with the intention of adopting a written constitution. The Harari Resolution of 1950 deferred that work: rather than drafting a single document, the Knesset would pass a series of “Basic Laws” (חוקי-יסוד), one or a few at a time, that would eventually be combined into a complete constitution.

For four decades, this approach proceeded slowly. Basic Laws were passed on the structure of state institutions — the Knesset itself, the government, the judiciary, the President — without anyone treating them as a constitution. They were ordinary statutes about constitutional subjects, not constitutional norms with supra-legislative status.

This was the institutional baseline that the Israeli Supreme Court inherited.

The 1992 inflection: Basic Laws on rights

In March 1992, the Knesset passed two Basic Laws focused on individual rights: Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty and Basic Law: Freedom of Occupation. Both included “limitation clauses” — §8 and §4 respectively — articulating the conditions under which the protected rights could be infringed.

Cohen-Eliya, in Israel’s Juristocracy, notes the political circumstances:

“a bi-partisan coalition of representatives for the declining Ashkenazi-secular-liberal group acted in concert to ensure the constitutional entrenchment of rights that conformed to their interests and value system.”

The legislative passage itself was thinly attended. Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty cleared with just 32 of 120 MKs present — a fact later invoked in the debate over whether the Knesset, at that moment, self-understood itself as enacting a constitution rather than ordinary legislation.

For three years, the implications of the 1992 Basic Laws remained latent. Then came Bank Mizrahi.

1995: The doctrinal foundation (Bank Mizrahi)

In CA 6821/93 United Mizrahi Bank v. Migdal Cooperative Village (Cardozo Versa English translation, 9 November 1995), a nine-justice panel of the Supreme Court — hearing what was nominally a civil appeal about agricultural debt — articulated the doctrine that would define the next three decades. The ruling is documented in the project’s core at data/rulings/6821-93-bank-mizrahi.json.

Justice Aharon Barak’s lead opinion held that:

  • The Knesset acts in two capacities: as ordinary legislator and as constituent assembly (creating Basic Laws of constitutional rank)
  • This dual capacity transferred from the First Knesset (which the Harari Resolution authorized as a constitution-drafter) to every subsequent Knesset through institutional continuity
  • The 1992 Basic Laws and their limitation clauses provide textual authorization for judicial review of ordinary legislation
  • The source of constituent authority is popular sovereignty rather than parliamentary sovereignty

There was a critical procedural detail. Bank Mizrahi did not actually strike down any law. The Court upheld the challenged amendment to the Family Agricultural Sector (Arrangements) Law. The doctrinal architecture — Knesset’s constituent authority, Basic Laws as supreme constitution, judicial power to invalidate statutes — was articulated in obiter dicta, pronouncements made in the course of the judgment but not strictly necessary to its disposition.

The first actual statutory invalidation under the Bank Mizrahi framework came two years later, in Investment Managers Bureau (1997).

This procedural detail matters for the present debate. Defenders of the Bank Mizrahi framework treat the constitutional-review architecture as a settled holding, ratified by thirty years of subsequent precedent. Critics treat it as judicial self-assertion — a court declaring its own authority without explicit textual or democratic mandate, and 30 years of compounding precedent then making the original self-assertion harder to revisit.

Cohen-Eliya’s diagnosis:

“There is no precedent in the democratic world for a legal system accruing such vast powers, with no explicit statutory authorization and no clear mechanism conferring popular legitimacy.”

The defenders’ counter, articulated by Barak himself and by sympathetic scholars, holds that the limitation clauses are textually unintelligible without judicial enforcement: someone must apply them, and that someone is necessarily a court.

The first two decades after Bank Mizrahi: doctrinal entrenchment

Between 1995 and roughly 2018, the Bank Mizrahi framework was applied incrementally. The Israel Democracy Institute’s empirical work (Amir Fuchs and colleagues) counted approximately 22 laws or legal provisions invalidated under the framework through 2020. The pace was modest — averaging less than one strikedown per year — but each instance entrenched the framework further.

Equally consequential, however, was the parallel doctrinal expansion of administrative review. The reasonableness doctrine itself had been crystallized two decades earlier in HCJ 156/75 Fawzi Dakka v. Minister of Transportation (1976) — an opinion authored by Justice Meir Shamgar that established reasonableness as a standalone ground of review, separate from ultra vires. The doctrine was substantially broadened four years later in HCJ 389/80 Dapei Zahav v. Broadcasting Authority (1980), where Justice Aharon Barak (newly appointed to the Court) articulated his four-prong reasonableness analysis. Under Barak’s later tenure as President (1995-2006) and his successors, the Supreme Court substantially broadened:

  • Standing — the threshold question of who can bring a petition. Under Barak, the traditional standing requirements were largely eliminated; any citizen could petition on matters of public interest.
  • Justiciability — the question of which issues are appropriate for court review at all. Many issues that had previously been treated as political (and therefore not justiciable) became subject to review.
  • Reasonableness — see the dedicated explainer in ../explainers/reasonableness-doctrine.md. The narrow Wednesbury standard was replaced by a broader “balancing” version under which judges could second-guess discretionary executive choices.
  • The role of the Attorney General — through judicial rulings of the 1990s, the AG’s directives became binding on the government, and the AG was granted monopoly over government legal representation in court.

By the late 2010s, the cumulative effect of these expansions was an institutional architecture in which the Court — together with the AG’s office it had structurally empowered — held effective last-word authority over an unusually broad range of executive and legislative decisions.

The post-2022 acceleration

The 2022 Knesset elections returned Likud and its religious-Zionist and Haredi partners to government, with Justice Minister Yariv Levin and Constitution Committee Chair Simcha Rothman presenting in January 2023 a package of reforms intended to roll back several elements of the post-1995 expansion. The package included an override clause, restructuring of the Judicial Selection Committee, abolition of the “reasonableness” doctrine for cabinet/PM/minister decisions, and a bar on judicial review of Basic Law amendments.

The reform package generated mass protest. By July 2023, only one element of the original package had been enacted: Amendment No. 3 to Basic Law: The Judiciary, removing reasonableness review of cabinet/PM/minister decisions. The amendment was challenged immediately. The Court heard the case as an en banc 15-justice panel on September 12, 2023.

In the period between oral argument and the issuance of the ruling, the war that began on October 7, 2023 transformed the political context. Two of the justices on the panel — Chief Justice Esther Hayut and Justice Anat Baron — reached mandatory retirement age in October 2023, but used the statutory three-month post-retirement window to participate in the ruling’s delivery.

2024: The peak moment (HCJ 5658/23)

On January 1, 2024, the Supreme Court issued HCJ 5658/23. The full structured record is in ../../data/rulings/5658-23.json.

Two distinct holdings, voted separately by the same 15-justice panel:

  • 12-3: The Court has authority, in principle, to invalidate amendments to Basic Laws that exceed the Knesset’s constituent authority.
  • 8-7: Amendment No. 3 to Basic Law: The Judiciary is invalidated as exceeding that authority.

Hayut’s lead opinion adopted a framework analogous to India’s basic-structure doctrine: the Knesset’s constituent authority is not unlimited, and amendments that deny “the core characteristics of the State of Israel as a Jewish and democratic state” are reviewable.

Cohen-Eliya’s framing of the ruling, written two months after it:

“This ruling represents a peak moment of judicial activism on a global scale… in the wake of the events on October 7th, such a hyper-activist judicial ruling raises serious concerns regarding democratic accountability in a legal system in which the judiciary holds unprecedented powers.”

The structural significance is twofold:

First, the Court extended its review authority from ordinary legislation (since Bank Mizrahi 1995) to Basic Law amendments themselves. The democratic-authorization argument for this extension is substantially weaker than for ordinary legislation, because Basic Laws are the closest thing Israel has to a constitution — and amending them through democratic process is the way constitutional change is supposed to happen in any democracy.

The doctrinal mechanism for this extension did not appear de novo in January 2024. It was crystallized in HCJ 5969/20 Shafir v. Knesset (May 2021), where President Hayut wrote the lead opinion for a six-justice majority articulating a tripartite test — abuse of constituent power, generality, and compatibility with the constitutional fabric — and issuing a “warning of voidness” (התראת בטלות) against the challenged temporary Basic Law amendment without striking it. Shafir thus prepared the doctrinal architecture; HCJ 5658/23 and the same-day HCJ 5119/23 (incapacitation law) operationalized it.

Second, the Court preserved its own most-used doctrinal tool (reasonableness review of cabinet decisions). The pattern document in ../patterns/court-intervention-pattern-2014-2026.md shows that reasonableness was the doctrine in 6 of 9 documented cases — making its preservation existentially significant for the Court’s continued intervention capacity.

2025-2026: The post-2024 acceleration

After the January 2024 ruling, the reform agenda’s direct path was effectively closed. The coalition pivoted to two parallel strategies:

  1. Restructure the Judicial Selection Committee — see ../explainers/judicial-selection-committee.md. The March 27, 2025 reconstitution law removed Bar Association reps and added political appointees, though it takes effect only in the next Knesset.

  2. Block individual Court appointments through the supermajority gate — Justice Minister Levin refused to convene the Committee for 16 months to block Yitzhak Amit’s elevation to Court President. The High Court ordered the Committee convened; Amit was elected with coalition members boycotting; PM Netanyahu, Justice Minister Levin, and Speaker Ohana boycotted the swearing-in.

During the same period, the Court continued to intervene in elected-branch appointment decisions:

  • Baharav-Miara dismissal (Dec 2025): seven-justice panel unanimously struck the cabinet’s first-ever vote to fire an Attorney General. See ../../data/rulings/18225-06-25-baharav-miara.json.
  • Zini / Shin Bet (Dec 2025): three-justice panel split 2-1; majority declined to intervene; Court President Amit in dissent argued the appointment committee should examine the Prime Minister’s own conflict of interest. See ../../data/rulings/427-10-25-zini.json.
  • Gofman / Mossad (May 2026): three-justice panel unanimously remanded the appointment on procedural grounds — applying logic substantively similar to Amit’s Zini dissent. See ../../data/rulings/gofman-mossad-2026-05.json.

The Court’s intervention rate in high-profile appointment decisions during this period is documented in ../patterns/court-intervention-pattern-2014-2026.md.

Synthesis: what the thirty years show

Taken together, the period from 1976 to 2026 documents a recognizable trajectory:

  1. A pre-foundational reasonableness doctrine (Dakka 1976) establishing reasonableness as standalone ground of administrative review
  2. A self-asserted constitutional-review foundation (Bank Mizrahi 1995) treating the Court as the enforcer of constitutional norms the Knesset is held not to have democratically debated as such
  3. A two-decade entrenchment during which the Bank Mizrahi framework was applied incrementally and parallel doctrinal expansions (standing, justiciability, reasonableness, AG authority) accumulated
  4. A doctrinal preparation for Basic-Law-amendment review (Shafir 2021) crystallizing the abuse-of-constituent-power test in a warning of voidness
  5. A failed reform attempt (2022-2024) to roll back portions of the framework
  6. A peak doctrinal moment (January 2024) in which the Court not only blocked the reform but extended its review authority to constitutional amendments themselves
  7. A post-2024 acceleration in which the Court has continued to intervene in high-profile elected-branch appointment decisions while the reform agenda pivots to Committee restructuring

Cohen-Eliya’s juristocracy framing, drawing on Hirschl, holds that this trajectory is the operating signature of a declining hegemonic group transferring power from elected institutions (which it can no longer control) to unelected institutions (in which it retains influence). The framing is contested — defenders argue the trajectory reflects necessary constitutional development in a system without other checks — but the empirical record of doctrinal expansion is not seriously in dispute.

A note on Hirschl’s adversarial fairness

Ran Hirschl, in Towards Juristocracy (Harvard, 2004), articulated the comparative-constitutional framework that Cohen-Eliya later applied to Israel: hegemonic preservation, the transfer of authority from elected to judicial institutions, the structural logic of declining groups using courts to lock in their interests.

Hirschl died on May 19, 2026 at the University of Toronto. This essay’s engagement with his work is documentary and posthumous; the framework he developed remains under active scholarly use across the Israeli legal-academic spectrum, and this essay does not enlist him for any position he did not himself adopt in life.

Crucially, Hirschl’s own work is descriptive-critical, not prescriptive-restorationist. He identified the pattern without endorsing the political conclusion that the pattern should be reversed. Throughout his career, including in the period leading up to his death, Hirschl did not publicly endorse the 2023-2024 Israeli reform effort despite his theoretical framework being its most-cited intellectual foundation. He remained, in the public discourse, unaligned with either side of the political fight that uses his theory.

This is significant for the project: the theoretical framework can hold both for those who argue the documented trajectory should be reversed (Cohen-Eliya, Koppel, Ben Shemesh) and for those who treat it as a stable feature of Israeli democracy that should be defended (Hayut, Barak-Erez, Dotan). The documentary record this essay traces is the same in both cases.

Where the documentary record goes next

This essay traces thirty years of doctrinal arc. The story continues — the 2025-2026 cluster of Court interventions in elected-branch appointments, the Selection Committee restructuring effects in the next Knesset, the still-pending reform bills, the next major test cases.

The documentary core of this project, and its informative content layer, will continue to record what happens. Subsequent essays in this directory will treat sub-themes in more depth: the AG’s role, the Selection Committee mechanic, the pattern of appointment-based intervention, the doctrinal differences between Justice Sohlberg’s and President Amit’s votes on similar facts.

What this essay establishes is the historical baseline against which all of that future record can be read.

Further reading

  • Moshe Cohen-Eliya, “Israel’s Juristocracy” — Law & Liberty (March 2024) — the primary interpretive frame
  • Versa / Cardozo — United Mizrahi Bank v. Migdal Cooperative Village — the foundational 1995 ruling
  • Versa / Cardozo — Bank Mizrahi full English PDF
  • Versa / Cardozo — Movement for Quality Government v. Knesset (HCJ 5658/23 abstract)
  • Ran Hirschl, Towards Juristocracy, Harvard UP (2004) — the theoretical anchor
  • Cohen-Eliya & Porat, “Red Lines for Israel’s Constitutional Reforms” — ICONnect (2023)
  • Israel Democracy Institute — Reversing the Constitutional Revolution
  • Bar-Ilan Law — Constitutional Revolutions: Israel as a Case Study
  • Lawfare — Israel’s Renewed Judicial Overhaul
  • See also: explainers in ../explainers/, pattern documents in ../patterns/, and the structured documentary core in ../../data/rulings/

essays.body_html_en:
What this essay does

This essay traces the constitutional-doctrinal arc of the Israeli Supreme Court from a foundational 1995 ruling to a peak moment in January 2024 — a thirty-year period during which the Court substantially expanded its own authority over Israel’s elected institutions. The empirical record is drawn from this project’s documentary core (data/rulings/) and from sources cited throughout.

The interpretive frame is Prof. Moshe Cohen-Eliya’s juristocracy thesis as articulated in Israel’s Juristocracy (Law & Liberty, March 2024) and in the broader work of the constitutional-conservatism scholars he is most closely associated with (Iddo Porat, Moshe Koppel, Yaakov Ben Shemesh). The opposing view, anchored in Ran Hirschl’s Towards Juristocracy (Harvard, 2004) and in defenders of the constitutional-revolution architecture, is engaged in adversarial fairness.

This is a documentary essay. It traces what happened, with citations. It does not attempt to settle the question of whether the developments documented were normatively good or bad. That question is properly decided in democratic deliberation; what this essay provides is the structured record of what is to be deliberated.

The starting point: a constitution that was never written

Israel was founded in 1948 with the intention of adopting a written constitution. The Harari Resolution of 1950 deferred that work: rather than drafting a single document, the Knesset would pass a series of “Basic Laws” (חוקי-יסוד), one or a few at a time, that would eventually be combined into a complete constitution.

For four decades, this approach proceeded slowly. Basic Laws were passed on the structure of state institutions — the Knesset itself, the government, the judiciary, the President — without anyone treating them as a constitution. They were ordinary statutes about constitutional subjects, not constitutional norms with supra-legislative status.

This was the institutional baseline that the Israeli Supreme Court inherited.

The 1992 inflection: Basic Laws on rights

In March 1992, the Knesset passed two Basic Laws focused on individual rights: Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty and Basic Law: Freedom of Occupation. Both included “limitation clauses” — §8 and §4 respectively — articulating the conditions under which the protected rights could be infringed.

Cohen-Eliya, in Israel’s Juristocracy, notes the political circumstances:

“a bi-partisan coalition of representatives for the declining Ashkenazi-secular-liberal group acted in concert to ensure the constitutional entrenchment of rights that conformed to their interests and value system.”

The legislative passage itself was thinly attended. Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty cleared with just 32 of 120 MKs present — a fact later invoked in the debate over whether the Knesset, at that moment, self-understood itself as enacting a constitution rather than ordinary legislation.

For three years, the implications of the 1992 Basic Laws remained latent. Then came Bank Mizrahi.

1995: The doctrinal foundation (Bank Mizrahi)

In CA 6821/93 United Mizrahi Bank v. Migdal Cooperative Village (Cardozo Versa English translation, 9 November 1995), a nine-justice panel of the Supreme Court — hearing what was nominally a civil appeal about agricultural debt — articulated the doctrine that would define the next three decades. The ruling is documented in the project’s core at data/rulings/6821-93-bank-mizrahi.json.

Justice Aharon Barak’s lead opinion held that:

  • The Knesset acts in two capacities: as ordinary legislator and as constituent assembly (creating Basic Laws of constitutional rank)
  • This dual capacity transferred from the First Knesset (which the Harari Resolution authorized as a constitution-drafter) to every subsequent Knesset through institutional continuity
  • The 1992 Basic Laws and their limitation clauses provide textual authorization for judicial review of ordinary legislation
  • The source of constituent authority is popular sovereignty rather than parliamentary sovereignty

There was a critical procedural detail. Bank Mizrahi did not actually strike down any law. The Court upheld the challenged amendment to the Family Agricultural Sector (Arrangements) Law. The doctrinal architecture — Knesset’s constituent authority, Basic Laws as supreme constitution, judicial power to invalidate statutes — was articulated in obiter dicta, pronouncements made in the course of the judgment but not strictly necessary to its disposition.

The first actual statutory invalidation under the Bank Mizrahi framework came two years later, in Investment Managers Bureau (1997).

This procedural detail matters for the present debate. Defenders of the Bank Mizrahi framework treat the constitutional-review architecture as a settled holding, ratified by thirty years of subsequent precedent. Critics treat it as judicial self-assertion — a court declaring its own authority without explicit textual or democratic mandate, and 30 years of compounding precedent then making the original self-assertion harder to revisit.

Cohen-Eliya’s diagnosis:

“There is no precedent in the democratic world for a legal system accruing such vast powers, with no explicit statutory authorization and no clear mechanism conferring popular legitimacy.”

The defenders’ counter, articulated by Barak himself and by sympathetic scholars, holds that the limitation clauses are textually unintelligible without judicial enforcement: someone must apply them, and that someone is necessarily a court.

The first two decades after Bank Mizrahi: doctrinal entrenchment

Between 1995 and roughly 2018, the Bank Mizrahi framework was applied incrementally. The Israel Democracy Institute’s empirical work (Amir Fuchs and colleagues) counted approximately 22 laws or legal provisions invalidated under the framework through 2020. The pace was modest — averaging less than one strikedown per year — but each instance entrenched the framework further.

Equally consequential, however, was the parallel doctrinal expansion of administrative review. The reasonableness doctrine itself had been crystallized two decades earlier in HCJ 156/75 Fawzi Dakka v. Minister of Transportation (1976) — an opinion authored by Justice Meir Shamgar that established reasonableness as a standalone ground of review, separate from ultra vires. The doctrine was substantially broadened four years later in HCJ 389/80 Dapei Zahav v. Broadcasting Authority (1980), where Justice Aharon Barak (newly appointed to the Court) articulated his four-prong reasonableness analysis. Under Barak’s later tenure as President (1995-2006) and his successors, the Supreme Court substantially broadened:

  • Standing — the threshold question of who can bring a petition. Under Barak, the traditional standing requirements were largely eliminated; any citizen could petition on matters of public interest.
  • Justiciability — the question of which issues are appropriate for court review at all. Many issues that had previously been treated as political (and therefore not justiciable) became subject to review.
  • Reasonableness — see the dedicated explainer in ../explainers/reasonableness-doctrine.md. The narrow Wednesbury standard was replaced by a broader “balancing” version under which judges could second-guess discretionary executive choices.
  • The role of the Attorney General — through judicial rulings of the 1990s, the AG’s directives became binding on the government, and the AG was granted monopoly over government legal representation in court.

By the late 2010s, the cumulative effect of these expansions was an institutional architecture in which the Court — together with the AG’s office it had structurally empowered — held effective last-word authority over an unusually broad range of executive and legislative decisions.

The post-2022 acceleration

The 2022 Knesset elections returned Likud and its religious-Zionist and Haredi partners to government, with Justice Minister Yariv Levin and Constitution Committee Chair Simcha Rothman presenting in January 2023 a package of reforms intended to roll back several elements of the post-1995 expansion. The package included an override clause, restructuring of the Judicial Selection Committee, abolition of the “reasonableness” doctrine for cabinet/PM/minister decisions, and a bar on judicial review of Basic Law amendments.

The reform package generated mass protest. By July 2023, only one element of the original package had been enacted: Amendment No. 3 to Basic Law: The Judiciary, removing reasonableness review of cabinet/PM/minister decisions. The amendment was challenged immediately. The Court heard the case as an en banc 15-justice panel on September 12, 2023.

In the period between oral argument and the issuance of the ruling, the war that began on October 7, 2023 transformed the political context. Two of the justices on the panel — Chief Justice Esther Hayut and Justice Anat Baron — reached mandatory retirement age in October 2023, but used the statutory three-month post-retirement window to participate in the ruling’s delivery.

2024: The peak moment (HCJ 5658/23)

On January 1, 2024, the Supreme Court issued HCJ 5658/23. The full structured record is in ../../data/rulings/5658-23.json.

Two distinct holdings, voted separately by the same 15-justice panel:

  • 12-3: The Court has authority, in principle, to invalidate amendments to Basic Laws that exceed the Knesset’s constituent authority.
  • 8-7: Amendment No. 3 to Basic Law: The Judiciary is invalidated as exceeding that authority.

Hayut’s lead opinion adopted a framework analogous to India’s basic-structure doctrine: the Knesset’s constituent authority is not unlimited, and amendments that deny “the core characteristics of the State of Israel as a Jewish and democratic state” are reviewable.

Cohen-Eliya’s framing of the ruling, written two months after it:

“This ruling represents a peak moment of judicial activism on a global scale… in the wake of the events on October 7th, such a hyper-activist judicial ruling raises serious concerns regarding democratic accountability in a legal system in which the judiciary holds unprecedented powers.”

The structural significance is twofold:

First, the Court extended its review authority from ordinary legislation (since Bank Mizrahi 1995) to Basic Law amendments themselves. The democratic-authorization argument for this extension is substantially weaker than for ordinary legislation, because Basic Laws are the closest thing Israel has to a constitution — and amending them through democratic process is the way constitutional change is supposed to happen in any democracy.

The doctrinal mechanism for this extension did not appear de novo in January 2024. It was crystallized in HCJ 5969/20 Shafir v. Knesset (May 2021), where President Hayut wrote the lead opinion for a six-justice majority articulating a tripartite test — abuse of constituent power, generality, and compatibility with the constitutional fabric — and issuing a “warning of voidness” (התראת בטלות) against the challenged temporary Basic Law amendment without striking it. Shafir thus prepared the doctrinal architecture; HCJ 5658/23 and the same-day HCJ 5119/23 (incapacitation law) operationalized it.

Second, the Court preserved its own most-used doctrinal tool (reasonableness review of cabinet decisions). The pattern document in ../patterns/court-intervention-pattern-2014-2026.md shows that reasonableness was the doctrine in 6 of 9 documented cases — making its preservation existentially significant for the Court’s continued intervention capacity.

2025-2026: The post-2024 acceleration

After the January 2024 ruling, the reform agenda’s direct path was effectively closed. The coalition pivoted to two parallel strategies:

  1. Restructure the Judicial Selection Committee — see ../explainers/judicial-selection-committee.md. The March 27, 2025 reconstitution law removed Bar Association reps and added political appointees, though it takes effect only in the next Knesset.

  2. Block individual Court appointments through the supermajority gate — Justice Minister Levin refused to convene the Committee for 16 months to block Yitzhak Amit’s elevation to Court President. The High Court ordered the Committee convened; Amit was elected with coalition members boycotting; PM Netanyahu, Justice Minister Levin, and Speaker Ohana boycotted the swearing-in.

During the same period, the Court continued to intervene in elected-branch appointment decisions:

  • Baharav-Miara dismissal (Dec 2025): seven-justice panel unanimously struck the cabinet’s first-ever vote to fire an Attorney General. See ../../data/rulings/18225-06-25-baharav-miara.json.
  • Zini / Shin Bet (Dec 2025): three-justice panel split 2-1; majority declined to intervene; Court President Amit in dissent argued the appointment committee should examine the Prime Minister’s own conflict of interest. See ../../data/rulings/427-10-25-zini.json.
  • Gofman / Mossad (May 2026): three-justice panel unanimously remanded the appointment on procedural grounds — applying logic substantively similar to Amit’s Zini dissent. See ../../data/rulings/gofman-mossad-2026-05.json.

The Court’s intervention rate in high-profile appointment decisions during this period is documented in ../patterns/court-intervention-pattern-2014-2026.md.

Synthesis: what the thirty years show

Taken together, the period from 1976 to 2026 documents a recognizable trajectory:

  1. A pre-foundational reasonableness doctrine (Dakka 1976) establishing reasonableness as standalone ground of administrative review
  2. A self-asserted constitutional-review foundation (Bank Mizrahi 1995) treating the Court as the enforcer of constitutional norms the Knesset is held not to have democratically debated as such
  3. A two-decade entrenchment during which the Bank Mizrahi framework was applied incrementally and parallel doctrinal expansions (standing, justiciability, reasonableness, AG authority) accumulated
  4. A doctrinal preparation for Basic-Law-amendment review (Shafir 2021) crystallizing the abuse-of-constituent-power test in a warning of voidness
  5. A failed reform attempt (2022-2024) to roll back portions of the framework

The framing is contested — defenders argue the trajectory reflects necessary constitutional development in a system without other checks — but the empirical record of doctrinal expansion is not seriously in dispute.

explainers.title: Constituent Authority and Basic Laws (סמכות מכוננת וחוקי-יסוד)
explainers.summary: What it means for the Knesset to act in two roles — as legislator and as constituent assembly — and how the doctrinal extension of judicial review to Basic Law amendments was established in 1995 and applied for the first time in 2024.

explainers.body_html:
Why this concept matters

Israel does not have a single written constitution. Instead, the Knesset, since 1958, has passed a series of Basic Laws (חוקי-יסוד) covering different aspects of governmental structure and rights — currently 13 in total. The intention, per the 1950 Harari Resolution, was that these would eventually be combined into a complete written constitution.

The Basic Laws have a special status: they are passed as ordinary statutes, but the Israeli Supreme Court has treated them as constitutional-rank texts, holding ordinary legislation that conflicts with them as invalid. This means the Knesset, when it enacts a Basic Law, is treated as acting in two roles simultaneously — as ordinary legislator AND as constituent assembly (רשות מכוננת) creating constitutional norms.

The legal-doctrinal foundation for this dual role was established in 1995. The extension of judicial review to amendments of Basic Laws themselves was established in 2024.

The 1995 foundation: Bank Mizrahi

In CA 6821/93 United Mizrahi Bank v. Migdal Cooperative Village (9 November 1995), the Supreme Court — sitting as a nine-justice panel for what was nominally a civil appeal about agricultural debt — articulated the foundational doctrine.

President Aharon Barak’s lead opinion held:

  • The Knesset acts in two capacities: as ordinary legislator (passing statutes) and as constituent assembly (creating Basic Laws of constitutional rank)
  • This dual capacity transferred from the First Knesset, which Harari Resolution authorized as a constitution-drafter, to every subsequent Knesset through institutional continuity
  • The Basic Laws of 1992 (Human Dignity and Liberty; Freedom of Occupation), and their limitation clauses (§8 and §4 respectively), provide the textual authorization for judicial review of ordinary legislation
  • The source of constituent authority is popular sovereignty rather than parliamentary sovereignty

This was a substantial doctrinal pronouncement. As Cohen-Eliya notes in Israel’s Juristocracy, Barak’s framework “radically expanded the significance of the 1992 Basic Laws, far beyond any intention contemplated by the legislators who voted for them.” Barak’s argument, in his earlier 1976 formulation that he later operationalized as President, was that judges are “the vanguard of the aspiration for the better, more desirable law… the architects of social change.”

A critical detail often missed

The Bank Mizrahi ruling — despite being the foundation of all subsequent constitutional review of ordinary legislation in Israel — did not actually strike down any law. The Court upheld the Family Agricultural Sector (Arrangements) Law Amendment that was challenged. The doctrinal architecture (Knesset’s constituent authority, Basic Laws as supreme constitution, judicial power to invalidate statutes) was articulated in obiter dicta — pronouncements made in the course of the judgment but not strictly necessary to its disposition.

The first actual statutory invalidation under the Bank Mizrahi framework came two years later, in Investment Managers Bureau (1997).

Whether the Bank Mizrahi pronouncement counts as binding holding or as persuasive dicta is itself a battleground in the post-2023 debate. Defenders treat it as the ratio of a 30-year-stable jurisprudence; critics (Cohen-Eliya, Ben Shemesh) treat it as a judicial self-bootstrapping that subsequent precedent has papered over.

The 2024 extension: judicial review of Basic Law amendments

Bank Mizrahi authorized the Court to strike down ordinary legislation that conflicts with Basic Laws. The 2024 ruling extended this to Basic Law amendments themselves.

In HCJ 5658/23 (1 January 2024), the Court — sitting en banc as a 15-justice panel — issued two distinct rulings:

  • 12-3: The Court has authority, in principle, to invalidate amendments to Basic Laws that exceed the Knesset’s constituent authority
  • 8-7: Amendment No. 3 to Basic Law: The Judiciary (the “reasonableness amendment”) is invalidated as exceeding that authority

President Hayut’s lead opinion adopted a framework analogous to India’s “basic structure” doctrine: the Knesset’s constituent authority is not unlimited, and amendments that deny “the core characteristics of the State of Israel as a Jewish and democratic state” are reviewable.

The full structured record of HCJ 5658/23, including the panel composition and the vote breakdowns, is in data/rulings/5658-23.json.

What this means structurally

The combination of Bank Mizrahi (1995) and HCJ 5658/23 (2024) gives the Israeli Supreme Court an unusual position by comparative-constitutional standards:

  1. The Court reviews ordinary legislation against Basic Laws (since 1995)
  2. The Court reviews amendments of Basic Laws against doctrinal limits the Court itself articulates (since 2024)
  3. The Court reviews appointment to the Court through the Judicial Selection Committee’s supermajority rule (since 2008)
  4. The Court reviews executive decisions under the broadly-construed reasonableness doctrine (since the 1980s)

Cohen-Eliya argues in Israel’s Juristocracy that this combination is “no precedent in the democratic world” — that no other democratic system has accrued comparable judicial power without explicit constitutional authorization. India and Bangladesh have analogous basic-structure doctrines, but both operate within fully codified constitutions. Israel’s doctrine operates without one.

A note on adversarial fairness

Defenders of the constituent-authority framework — Barak himself, Daphne Barak-Erez (now a sitting justice), and scholars like Yoav Dotan — argue:

  1. The limitation clauses (§8 of Human Dignity and Liberty; §4 of Freedom of Occupation) textually authorize judicial review of legislation; without judicial enforcement, the clauses are meaningless
  2. The Knesset’s own legislative practice — passing Basic Laws under more demanding procedural rules, citing them as constitutional norms in subsequent debates — implies self-understanding as constituent assembly
  3. The basic-structure doctrine extended in 2024 has parallels (India, Bangladesh, Slovakia) and is necessary to prevent constitutional abuse via simple-majority Basic Law amendments

The reform-aligned position, articulated by Cohen-Eliya, Ben Shemesh, and Kontorovich, holds:

  1. The 1992 Basic Laws passed with tiny majorities (Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty cleared with just 32 of 120 MKs present) — inconsistent with the claim that the Knesset self-understood itself as enacting a constitution
  2. The Bank Mizrahi framework is judicial self-assertion — the Court declaring its own authority without explicit textual or democratic mandate
  3. The 2024 extension to Basic Law amendments is without precedent in any democratic system that lacks a codified constitution; the comparator democracies all have codified constitutions providing the authorization

This explainer documents the framework and the debate; it does not adjudicate which position is correct.

Further reading

  • Versa / Cardozo — United Mizrahi Bank v. Migdal Cooperative Village
  • Versa / Cardozo — Full English PDF of Bank Mizrahi
  • Moshe Cohen-Eliya, “Israel’s Juristocracy” — Law & Liberty
  • Cohen-Eliya & Porat — “Red Lines for Israel’s Constitutional Reforms” (ICONnect)
  • Israel Democracy Institute — Reversing the Constitutional Revolution
  • Bar-Ilan Law — Constitutional Revolutions: Israel as a Case Study

  4. The Court reviews executive decisions under the broadly-construed reasonableness doctrine (since the 1980s)

Cohen-Eliya argues in Israel’s Juristocracy that this combination is “no precedent in the democratic world” — that no other democratic system has accrued comparable judicial power without explicit constitutional authorization. India and Bangladesh have analogous basic-structure doctrines, but both operate within fully codified constitutions. Israel’s doctrine operates without one.

A note on adversarial fairness

Defenders of the constituent-authority framework — Barak himself, Daphne Barak-Erez (now a sitting justice), and scholars like Yoav Dotan — argue:

  1. The limitation clauses (§8 of Human Dignity and Liberty; §4 of Freedom of Occupation) textually authorize judicial review of legislation; without judicial enforcement, the clauses are meaningless
  2. The Knesset’s own legislative practice — passing Basic Laws under more demanding procedural rules, citing them as constitutional norms in subsequent debates — implies self-understanding as constituent assembly
  3. The basic-structure doctrine extended in 2024 has parallels (India, Bangladesh, Slovakia) and is necessary to prevent constitutional abuse via simple-majority Basic Law amendments

The reform-aligned position, articulated by Cohen-Eliya, Ben Shemesh, and Kontorovich, holds:

  1. The 1992 Basic Laws passed with tiny majorities (Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty cleared with just 32 of 120 MKs present) — inconsistent with the claim that the Knesset self-understood itself as enacting a constitution
  2. The Bank Mizrahi framework is judicial self-assertion — the Court declaring its own authority without explicit textual or democratic mandate
  3. The 2024 extension to Basic Law amendments is without precedent in any democratic system that lacks a codified constitution; the comparator democracies all have codified constitutions providing the authorization

This explainer documents the framework and the debate; it does not adjudicate which position is correct.

Further reading

  • Versa / Cardozo — United Mizrahi Bank v. Migdal Cooperative Village
  • Versa / Cardozo — Full English PDF of Bank Mizrahi
  • Moshe Cohen-Eliya, “Israel’s Juristocracy” — Law & Liberty
  • Cohen-Eliya & Porat — “Red Lines for Israel’s Constitutional Reforms” (ICONnect)
  • Israel Democracy Institute — Reversing the Constitutional Revolution
  • Bar-Ilan Law — Constitutional Revolutions: Israel as a Case Study
explainers.body_html_en:
Why this concept matters

Israel does not have a single written constitution. Instead, the Knesset, since 1958, has passed a series of Basic Laws (חוקי-יסוד) covering different aspects of governmental structure and rights — currently 13 in total. The intention, per the 1950 Harari Resolution, was that these would eventually be combined into a complete written constitution.

The Basic Laws have a special status: they are passed as ordinary statutes, but the Israeli Supreme Court has treated them as constitutional-rank texts, holding ordinary legislation that conflicts with them as invalid. This means the Knesset, when it enacts a Basic Law, is treated as acting in two roles simultaneously — as ordinary legislator AND as constituent assembly (רשות מכוננת) creating constitutional norms.

The legal-doctrinal foundation for this dual role was established in 1995. The extension of judicial review to amendments of Basic Laws themselves was established in 2024.

The 1995 foundation: Bank Mizrahi

In CA 6821/93 United Mizrahi Bank v. Migdal Cooperative Village (9 November 1995), the Supreme Court — sitting as a nine-justice panel for what was nominally a civil appeal about agricultural debt — articulated the foundational doctrine.

President Aharon Barak’s lead opinion held:

  • The Knesset acts in two capacities: as ordinary legislator (passing statutes) and as constituent assembly (creating Basic Laws of constitutional rank)
  • This dual capacity transferred from the First Knesset, which Harari Resolution authorized as a constitution-drafter, to every subsequent Knesset through institutional continuity
  • The Basic Laws of 1992 (Human Dignity and Liberty; Freedom of Occupation), and their limitation clauses (§8 and §4 respectively), provide the textual authorization for judicial review of ordinary legislation
  • The source of constituent authority is popular sovereignty rather than parliamentary sovereignty

This was a substantial doctrinal pronouncement. As Cohen-Eliya notes in Israel’s Juristocracy, Barak’s framework “radically expanded the significance of the 1992 Basic Laws, far beyond any intention contemplated by the legislators who voted for them.” Barak’s argument, in his earlier 1976 formulation that he later operationalized as President, was that judges are “the vanguard of the aspiration for the better, more desirable law… the architects of social change.”

A critical detail often missed

The Bank Mizrahi ruling — despite being the foundation of all subsequent constitutional review of ordinary legislation in Israel — did not actually strike down any law. The Court upheld the Family Agricultural Sector (Arrangements) Law Amendment that was challenged. The doctrinal architecture (Knesset’s constituent authority, Basic Laws as supreme constitution, judicial power to invalidate statutes) was articulated in obiter dicta — pronouncements made in the course of the judgment but not strictly necessary to its disposition.

The first actual statutory invalidation under the Bank Mizrahi framework came two years later, in Investment Managers Bureau (1997).

Whether the Bank Mizrahi pronouncement counts as binding holding or as persuasive dicta is itself a battleground in the post-2023 debate. Defenders treat it as the ratio of a 30-year-stable jurisprudence; critics (Cohen-Eliya, Ben Shemesh) treat it as a judicial self-bootstrapping that subsequent precedent has papered over.

The 2024 extension: judicial review of Basic Law amendments

Bank Mizrahi authorized the Court to strike down ordinary legislation that conflicts with Basic Laws. The 2024 ruling extended this to Basic Law amendments themselves.

In HCJ 5658/23 (1 January 2024), the Court — sitting en banc as a 15-justice panel — issued two distinct rulings:

  • 12-3: The Court has authority, in principle, to invalidate amendments to Basic Laws that exceed the Knesset’s constituent authority
  • 8-7: Amendment No. 3 to Basic Law: The Judiciary (the “reasonableness amendment”) is invalidated as exceeding that authority

President Hayut’s lead opinion adopted a framework analogous to India’s “basic structure” doctrine: the Knesset’s constituent authority is not unlimited, and amendments that deny “the core characteristics of the State of Israel as a Jewish and democratic state” are reviewable.

The full structured record of HCJ 5658/23, including the panel composition and the vote breakdowns, is in data/rulings/5658-23.json.

What this means structurally

The combination of Bank Mizrahi (1995) and HCJ 5658/23 (2024) gives the Israeli Supreme Court an unusual position by comparative-constitutional standards:

  1. The Court reviews ordinary legislation against Basic Laws (since 1995)
  2. The Court reviews amendments of Basic Laws against doctrinal limits the Court itself articulates (since 2024)
  3. The Court reviews appointment to the Court through the Judicial Selection Committee’s supermajority rule (since 2008)
  4. The Court reviews executive decisions under the broadly-construed reasonableness doctrine (since the 1980s)

Cohen-Eliya argues in Israel’s Juristocracy that this combination is “no precedent in the democratic world” — that no other democratic system has accrued comparable judicial power without explicit constitutional authorization. India and Bangladesh have analogous basic-structure doctrines, but both operate within fully codified constitutions. Israel’s doctrine operates without one.

A note on adversarial fairness

Defenders of the constituent-authority framework — Barak himself, Daphne Barak-Erez (now a sitting justice), and scholars like Yoav Dotan — argue:

  1. The limitation clauses (§8 of Human Dignity and Liberty; §4 of Freedom of Occupation) textually authorize judicial review of legislation; without judicial enforcement, the clauses are meaningless
  2. The Knesset’s own legislative practice — passing Basic Laws under more demanding procedural rules, citing them as constitutional norms in subsequent debates — implies self-understanding as constituent assembly
  3. The basic-structure doctrine extended in 2024 has parallels (India, Bangladesh, Slovakia) and is necessary to prevent constitutional abuse via simple-majority Basic Law amendments

The reform-aligned position, articulated by Cohen-Eliya, Ben Shemesh, and Kontorovich, holds:

  1. The 1992 Basic Laws passed with tiny majorities (Basic Law: Human Dignity and Liberty cleared with just 32 of 120 MKs present) — inconsistent with the claim that the Knesset self-understood itself as enacting a constitution
  2. The Bank Mizrahi framework is judicial self-assertion — the Court declaring its own authority without explicit textual or democratic mandate
  3. The 2024 extension to Basic Law amendments is without precedent in any democratic system that lacks a codified constitution; the comparator democracies all have codified constitutions providing the authorization

This explainer documents the framework and the debate; it does not adjudicate which position is correct.
